questions.exp: COMPREHENSIVE = covering all or nearly all aspects. Example usage from the editorial: the health survey is described as a comprehensive survey.
REIMBURSEMENT = money paid back for expenses. Example usage from the editorial: PMJAY reimbursement rates are discussed.
ANCILLARY = supplementary or supporting. Example usage from the editorial: private hospitals bill separately for ancillary services.
CATASTROPHIC = causing great damage or suffering. Example usage from the editorial: rising catastrophic health costs are mentioned.
DECORUM = dignified and proper conduct. Example usage from the editorial: coalition politics requires respecting decorum.
VIABLE = capable of working successfully. Example usage from the editorial: the UDF must remain viable amid social change.
UPHEAVALS = major disruptions or disturbances. Example usage from the editorial: global upheavals are linked to political shifts.
IMPEDIMENTS = things that block or hinder progress. Example usage from the editorial: liberal norms are vilified as impediments.
COLLUDED = worked together secretly, usually for a wrong purpose. Example usage from the editorial: the Right is said to have colluded internationally.
OBFUSCATE = to make something unclear or difficult to understand. Example usage from the editorial: efforts to obfuscate targeting are mentioned.
DUBIOUS = doubtful or not reliable. Example usage from the editorial: exit polls of dubious authority are mentioned.
RETICENCE = unwillingness to speak or act openly. Example usage from the editorial: India’s reticence is discussed in foreign policy context.
UNFETTERED = free from restrictions. Example usage from the editorial: Abu Dhabi is described as having unfettered itself from quota restrictions.
ANODYNE = unlikely to offend; bland or harmless. Example usage from the editorial: the UAE statement contains anodyne references.

questions.q: Idiom: **'counting its chickens before they hatch'** means:
questions.exp: The idiom means assuming success before it actually happens. Example usage from the editorial: Congress leaders are described as counting their chickens before they hatch.
The idiom means to make something more confused or complicated. Example usage from the editorial: an ally’s statement muddied the waters in Kerala politics.

options: Gain an advantage secretly or early
questions.exp: The idiom means to gain an advantage over others. Example usage from the editorial: the UAE aims to steal a march over Iran and Saudi Arabia.
TAKE DOWN = remove, delete or block content. Example usage from the editorial: platforms are asked to take down online content.
PUT A LID ON = control, limit or suppress something. Example usage from the editorial: Congress tried to put a lid on public ambition.
LOOK DEEPER = examine something more carefully. Example usage from the editorial: analysts are prompted to look deeper into the UAE’s OPEC exit.
